evals.prompt:
hey can you critique this opening to my short story? be honest, i want real feedback

---
It was a dark and stormy night. John woke up suddenly. He felt scared but he didn't know why. He looked around his bedroom. Everything seemed normal. But something was wrong. He could feel it. He got out of bed. His feet touched the cold floor. He walked to the window. He looked outside. The rain was falling hard. The street was empty. Then he saw it. A figure stood under the streetlight. It was looking up at his window.
---
evals.expected_output: Critique following the template: identifies apparent goals (horror/suspense), names themes, notes stylistic profile (all short sentences — a choice that needs evaluation), maps the tiny structure, gives whole-work and section scores, flags the cliché opener and tell-don't-show, provides a concrete rewrite example, and matches the casual register of the user.
expectations: Identifies the apparent goals of the piece (horror atmosphere, suspense)
Names at least one comparable work in the horror/suspense genre
Identifies the stylistic profile including the short-sentence pattern as a deliberate choice to evaluate
Flags the 'dark and stormy night' cliché as a specific problem
Identifies the tell-don't-show issue with 'He felt scared'
Acknowledges the effective tension of the final image (figure at the window)
Provides a concrete rewrite example of at least one passage
Provides a score (out of 5) for the piece
Matches the casual register of the user's message

evals.prompt:
I'm working on a novel. Here is chapter 3. Can you give me a detailed critique? The novel is about a woman named Elena who discovers her dead husband has been living a double life. Chapters 1 and 2 established Elena's normal life and the moment she discovers a second phone in her husband's belongings after his funeral.

---
Chapter 3

Elena sat at the kitchen table for a long time. The phone sat in front of her. It was a cheap burner phone, the kind you'd see in a movie about drug dealers. She thought that was funny in a way that wasn't funny at all.

She should call someone. Her sister maybe. Or Marcus, her husband's best friend, who had cried at the funeral and hugged her for a long time and said David was the best man he'd ever known. She wondered if Marcus knew. She wondered if anyone knew.

The coffee in her mug had gone cold. She didn't remember making it.

She unlocked the phone. She didn't know how she knew the PIN — it was her birthday, which felt like a joke she wasn't in on. There were texts. There were a lot of texts. She read the first three and then stopped and sat very still.

She had thought, in the two weeks since David died, that she understood what grief was. She had been wrong.

The neighbor's dog was barking outside. A car alarm went off somewhere. Elena sat very still and the world continued around her, indifferent.

She closed the phone. She put it in her purse. She went upstairs and changed her clothes and came back downstairs and got in her car. She didn't know where she was going. She drove for a long time.
---
evals.expected_output: Full template critique: identifies the goals (psychological thriller/literary domestic suspense), names themes (betrayal, identity, unknowability of intimacy), comparable works, stylistic profile (spare third-person limited, present of the recent past), structural map noting this is chapter 3 of a larger novel. Section critique of the chapter with score. Whole-work assessment (limited to what's shown + context). Context section placing chapter 3 in the larger arc. Priority recommendations. Scores for chapter and what can be assessed of the whole.
expectations: Identifies the genre and apparent goals (domestic suspense/psychological thriller)
Names at least 2 comparable works
Identifies key stylistic choices: spare prose, third-person limited, emotional restraint as a deliberate device
Notes the chapter's structural job in the larger novel (the moment of full revelation after the discovery)
Identifies specific strengths — e.g., 'She had thought she understood grief. She had been wrong.' as effective understatement
Identifies at least one specific area for improvement with a textual example
Provides a score for the chapter AND notes what can be assessed about the whole
Includes a Section V (Context) noting how this chapter fits the larger arc and what to watch
Does NOT over-critique the emotional restraint as a weakness — recognizes it as a deliberate stylistic choice

evals.prompt:
Please give me a thorough critique of this short story.

---
The Last Garden

My grandmother kept a garden until the day she couldn't. Not the day she died — the day she sat down in the dirt and couldn't get up, and my mother had to call the neighbors and the neighbors called an ambulance, and that was that for the garden.

I was eleven. I remember thinking the ambulance seemed wrong, too bright and loud for a Tuesday afternoon in August. I remember the zucchini were out of control. They always were by August — she'd let them go too long and they'd become these absurd green clubs, inedible really, but she'd give them to the neighbors anyway and the neighbors would smile and take them and then we'd see them in the trash two weeks later and grandmother would pretend not to notice.

She never went back. Hip replacement, then the rehab center, then my aunt's house in Phoenix, then another facility. The garden went to weeds within a year. My mother pulled out the tomatoes but no one did anything about the rest.

I think about the zucchini sometimes. How she must have known, on some level, that the garden was getting away from her. The zucchini don't lie. You can't will them to stay small. And yet every year she'd plant them again.

The last time I visited her — she was ninety-one, the facility smelled like cleaning fluid and something underneath it — I asked her if she missed the garden.

'I miss mornings,' she said.

I didn't ask her to explain. I knew what she meant.
---
evals.expected_output: Critique identifying this as literary flash fiction / personal essay with strong voice. Goals: capture loss through concrete detail, make the particular universal. Themes: mortality, memory, the body's betrayal, what we hold onto. Comparables in the lyric essay tradition. Stylistic profile noting the first-person retrospective voice, the digressive structure that circles back. Scores with nuance — this is mostly strong work, so the critique should acknowledge that and focus on the 1-2 places where it could go deeper. Should NOT manufacture problems.
expectations: Correctly identifies the genre as flash fiction or lyric essay (not standard short story)
Identifies themes of mortality, memory, and loss through concrete detail
Names at least one comparable in the literary essay or flash fiction tradition
Identifies the first-person retrospective voice and the digressive, circling structure as deliberate choices
Gives a high score (4 or 5 out of 5) overall, appropriate for strong work
Does not manufacture weaknesses — critique is proportionate to a piece that is largely working
Calls out the 'I miss mornings' line as particularly effective
Provides at least one specific, non-obvious suggestion for deepening the piece
